sanskrit_devanagari:
य एनं वेत्ति हन्तारं यश्चैनं मन्यते हतम् ।
उभौ तौ न विजानीतो नायं हन्ति न हन्यते ॥ १९ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
ya enaṁ vetti hantāraṁ yaś cainaṁ manyate hatam |
ubhau tau na vijānīto nāyaṁ hanti na hanyate || 19 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical)
exact (academic critical edition; Bhg_02.019 = MBh_06,024.019). Body text identical.

translations.verbatim_quote: Neither he who thinks the living entity the slayer nor he who thinks it slain is in knowledge, for the self slays not nor is slain.
Neither of them is in knowledge—the one who thinks the soul can slay and the one who thinks the soul can be slain. For truly, the soul neither kills nor can it be killed.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt, ~285 chars of 860-char full purport)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: When an embodied living entity is hurt by fatal weapons, it is to be known that the living entity within the body is not killed. The spirit soul is so small that it is impossible to kill him by any material weapon, as will be evident from subsequent verses.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (per Advaita Ashrama edition; bhagavad-gita.us reproduces this English rendering of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: But the ideas that you have, 'Bhisma and others are being killed by me in war; I am surely their killer' — this idea of yours is false. How? Yah, he who; vetti, thinks; of enam, this One, the embodied One under consideration; as hantaram, the killer, the agent of the act of killing.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the verse non-dually: at the level of the Self, there is no agent of killing and no patient of killing. Both the idea 'I am the slayer' and the idea 'this one is being slain' attribute action and patiency to the Self, which has neither. The verse is essentially the Kaṭha Upaniṣad 1.2.19 quoted in the Gita; Shankara's bhasya cross-references the Upaniṣad explicitly. Action belongs to the body and the gunas, not to the Self.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (per Sri Ramakrishna Math edition; bhagavad-gita.us reproduces this English rendering of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: With regard to 'This' viz., the self, whose nature has been described above, he who thinks of It as the slayer, i.e., as the cause of slaying, and he who thinks 'This' (self) as slain by some cause or other — both of them do not know. As this self is eternal for the reasons mentioned above.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads the verse as preserving the embodied jīva: the jīva is not the agent of killing (the body's act, mediated by the senses, is) and is not the patient of killing (the body's destruction does not destroy the jīva). The jīva remains as the eternal embodied; what changes is the body. The Vishishtadvaita reading preserves the jīva's individuality where Advaita's reading dissolves it into the universal Self.

positions.rendering: the embodied = the universal Self; the verse asserts non-doership at the level of Self
the embodied = the localised jīva; the jīva is neither agent nor patient of killing

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The Advaita reading dissolves the agent: the Self is not a doer at all. The Vishishtadvaita reading preserves the jīva but denies that the jīva is the doer of bodily acts (the body and gunas are). Both readings agree that 'I killed Bhīṣma' is metaphysically wrong — they disagree on whose 'I' is doing the wrong attribution. The engineering layer should stay inside the consensus (the spec is not slain when the implementation is) without picking either ontological side.

positions.rendering: is not in knowledge / does not know
fails to discriminate Self from non-Self (viveka)
fails to know the difference between jīva and the body's acts

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: vijānīta is the technical term for discriminating knowledge — the kind of knowing that makes the right cuts between substrate and modification. Both bhasyas read the verse as a charge of avivekitā (lack of discrimination), not mere ignorance.

literal_meaning: Whoever thinks this one the slayer, and whoever thinks this one slain — both do not know. This one neither slays nor is slain.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The verse — quoted nearly verbatim from Kaṭha Upaniṣad 1.2.19 — denies both agency and patiency to the embodied. The one who thinks 'I am the killer' and the one who thinks 'I am being killed' both fail to know. The doctrinal scope of 'the embodied' differs across traditions (Advaita: the universal Self; Vishishtadvaita: the localised jīva), but the negation is shared: the embodied is neither the agent nor the patient of killing. Action and being-acted-upon belong to the body and its gunas; what is real about the actor is undisturbed. The verse is thus the cleanest statement in the chapter that the substrate of the actor is unaffected by the action.
